persona_updates.new_value: recent breakup, new project at work, comment about weight, anxiety about performance review

evidence_citations.excerpt: Every time I look at the expense report, I just stall and think about the split.
The breakup still feels like an unfinished project that keeps leaking into my work tasks.
The review feels like I'm being judged on something that feels out of my control
I keep deferring tasks as a way to avoid that reality.
I keep deferring it, and it feels like I'm stuck in a loop of procrastination.
I'm stuck in a loop of procrastination

transcript.text: Every time I look at the expense report, I just stall and think about the split. The financial strain feels like a reminder that I can't control anything, and I keep deferring tasks as a way to avoid that reality.

When you're faced with the expense report, it's like the financial strain hits a bit too close to home, reminding you of the split and making you feel like you're losing control. It's really tough to see a reminder of something painful every time you try to tackle a task. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd stall and try to avoid that feeling. It sounds like the expense report has become a trigger that sets off a lot of emotions and self-doubt for you.

Yeah, the expense report feels like a project that keeps getting delayed because the numbers keep reminding me of the split. I keep deferring it, and it feels like I'm stuck in a loop of procrastination. It's like a life transition that I can't manage.

The expense report has become a constant reminder of the split, and it's like every time you try to work on it, you're faced with a mix of emotions and self-doubt. It's not just about the numbers; it's about the life transition you're struggling to manage. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd feel stuck in a loop of procrastination when every task feels like a reminder of what you're trying to move past.

I keep seeing the expense report as a sprint that keeps getting delayed, like a backlog item that just keeps piling up. I want to set a concrete deadline, but the emotional weight of the breakup keeps pulling the task off the board. Maybe if I break it into smaller chunks it'll feel more manageable.

You're feeling overwhelmed by the expense report, and it's not just about the numbers - it's about the emotional weight of the breakup that's still lingering. Breaking down the task into smaller chunks might help make it feel more manageable, and I'm here to support you in exploring that idea. What's the smallest step you could take today to start moving forward with the expense report?

I'll open the expense file, flag the top three categories, and schedule a 30‑minute block in my calendar to sort them. That should make it feel less like a looming project and more like a series of quick tasks. I still feel stuck, but this is a concrete first move.

You've taken a really important first step by deciding to open the expense file and schedule a block of time to work on it. That takes a lot of courage, especially when the task feels overwhelming. Yeah, breaking it down into smaller chunks can make a big difference - what do you think might happen if you focus on just those top three categories you mentioned, and see how that goes before moving on to the rest?

I could try it, but I'm not sure the numbers will stay the same after I finish. There's a chance I'll get stuck again, and that feels like I'm failing both at work and in my personal life. I keep wondering if this will just push the breakup memories back into the foreground.

You're worried that even if you make progress on the expense report, the numbers might not stay the same, and that could lead to feeling stuck again. It's like the fear of failure is looming over you, and it's not just about the task itself, but also about the memories of the breakup that it brings up. That can be really daunting. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd wonder if this will all just lead to more pain and struggle.

I keep thinking the numbers will shift after I adjust, so I keep stalling—it's like a sprint that never ends. The expense report feels like a moving target that keeps me looping back to the breakup. I need a concrete deadline, but the fear of the numbers changing makes it feel like another life transition I can't control.

The expense report feels like a constant reminder of the breakup, and the fear of the numbers changing is holding you back. It's like you're trying to tackle a moving target, and that's really unsettling. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd feel stuck in this loop, especially when every task feels like a reminder of what you're trying to move past. What's the smallest step you could take today to start moving forward with the expense report, despite the fear of those numbers changing?
I could set a 15‑minute timer to just look at the top three categories, but I'm still worried the numbers will shift after I close the file. This feels like another life transition I can't control.

You're worried that the numbers will shift after you close the file, and that's making it hard for you to take the first step. It's like you're waiting for a sense of control or certainty before you can move forward. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd feel that way, especially when everything feels like it's in flux right now. Setting a 15-minute timer to just look at the top three categories might be a good place to start - what do you think would happen if you just focused on that one small task, without worrying about the rest?
I'll set the timer, but the numbers still feel like a moving target—it's like a sprint that never ends.

You're really struggling with the idea that the numbers might change, and that's making it hard for you to feel like you're making progress on the expense report. It's like you're trying to hit a moving target, and that's frustrating and unsettling. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd feel that way, especially when everything else in your life feels like it's in flux right now. Can you tell me more about what's driving that sense of uncertainty for you?

The uncertainty comes from the expense report being a moving target; I don't know how the numbers will settle, and that feeds into my anxiety about the upcoming performance review. The breakup still feels like an unfinished project that keeps leaking into my work tasks.

The uncertainty of the expense report and the performance review is really getting to you, and it's understandable that it's triggering anxiety. It sounds like the breakup is still feeling really raw, and it's leaking into your work tasks in a way that's making it hard for you to focus. Yeah, it makes sense that you'd feel overwhelmed by all of this - it's like you're trying to navigate a lot of different challenges at once. Can you tell me more about what's been the hardest part of dealing with the expense report and the performance review?

The hardest part is that every time I open the expense file, the numbers shift and I feel like I'm back at the breakup, so I keep stalling. The review feels like I'm being judged on something that feels out of my control, and I'm stuck in a loop of procrastination.

The expense report feels like a trigger that brings back memories of the breakup, and the constant changes in numbers make you feel like you're losing control. It's frustrating when something that's supposed to be a routine task turns into a source of anxiety. You're not just dealing with the report itself, but also the emotions it stirs up. Can you think of a small way to approach the report that might make it feel less overwhelming?

common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis

common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning

common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure

common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan
